dialogues: You read and created bar graphs with scale of 10. You figured out values at the lines AND between the lines. Let's look at some patterns you discovered.
Look at all four graphs. They show the same amount: 20. Which scale uses the fewest lines to show the same amount?
Right. Scale of 10 uses the fewest lines. Each line shows more, so you need fewer of them.
You've learned four scales now: 1, 2, 5, and 10. Each one is useful for different sized numbers. Bigger numbers work better with bigger scales.
Here's the same data shown two ways, vertical and horizontal. Find the category with 35 in BOTH graphs.
Now find 35 in the horizontal graph.
That's it. Same value, same position between the lines, just different directions. The axis works the same way whether bars go up or sideways. The math doesn't change, only the direction.
Scale of 10 is useful when numbers are bigger. Where might you see counting by 10s outside of math class?
Right. Money and measuring often use 10s. That's why scale of 10 feels natural for bigger amounts.
You already know how to count by 10s. Bar graphs use the same skill you use with money and measuring.
This bar shows 45, between the lines. How did you figure that out during the lesson?
Right. Half of 10 is 5, so halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower line.
Knowing that pattern means you can read any bar graph with scale of 10, even values between the lines. That's a powerful strategy.
You learned to read values at the lines and between the lines. You used 'half of 10 is 5' to figure out tricky values.
That pattern-finding will help you when we look at all the scales together next time. You'll be able to pick the best one for any data.
